definition: A substance that releases hydrogen ions (H⁺) when dissolved in water. Acids taste sour, can be corrosive, and react with metals to produce hydrogen gas.

An acid that completely dissociates in water, releasing all its hydrogen ions. The three most common are hydrochloric acid (HCl), sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) and nitric acid (HNO₃). Highly corrosive and must be handled with protective equipment.

An acid that only partially dissociates in water. Examples include citric acid, acetic acid and lactic acid, which are common in food and drink. Weak acids are milder but can still be corrosive in concentrated form.
Hydrogen chloride (HCl) dissolved in water – a strong, corrosive acid. It occurs naturally in gastric juice and is widely used in industry and laboratories.

H₂SO₄ – one of the most important industrial chemicals. Concentrated sulphuric acid is extremely corrosive and absorbs water from organic materials. Rule: always add acid to water (never the reverse).
H⁺ ions released when an acid dissolves in water. These ions make a solution acidic. The more hydrogen ions present, the lower the pH and the more acidic the solution.
A substance whose aqueous solution contains hydroxide ions (OH⁻). Bases are the opposite of acids – they feel slippery, can be corrosive, and are used in cleaning products and soap.
OH⁻ ions that make a solution alkaline. The more hydroxide ions present, the higher the pH and the more basic the solution.
NaOH – a strongly alkaline, white solid (caustic soda). When dissolved in water it forms a corrosive solution used in drain cleaning, paper manufacture and soap making.
The reaction when an acid and a base are mixed and H⁺ and OH⁻ cancel each other out. The result is water and a salt. A neutral solution is neither acidic nor basic.

A scale from 0 to 14 that measures how acidic or basic a solution is. pH 7 is neutral; below 7 is acidic; above 7 is basic. Each step on the scale represents a tenfold change in acidity or alkalinity.

A substance that changes colour depending on the pH of a solution. Litmus turns red in acid and blue in alkali. BTB is yellow in acid, green when neutral and blue in alkali. Universal indicator shows a pH scale of colours.

A safety rule for diluting concentrated acids: always add acid to water, never water to acid. If water is added to acid, the heat released can cause the solution to boil violently and splash dangerously.
